file_name: Saha et al. - 2025 - SkinFLNet a federated learning approach for skin cancer detection utilizing skin dermoscopy images.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically five Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet169, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2) combined with a Federated Learning approach, to classify skin cancer from dermoscopy images. The best model (FL-VGG16) achieved 92.08% accuracy on the ISBI2016 dataset. Explainable AI (LIME) was also used for interpretation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel federated learning approach (SkinFLNet) for skin cancer detection. It details original experimental methods using five Deep CNN models on the ISBI2016 dataset, reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 92.08% accuracy), and includes sections for Methods, Experiments, Results, and Discussion, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a review.

title: SkinFLNet: A Federated Learning Approach for Skin Cancer Detection Utilizing Skin Dermoscopy Images
brief_summary: This study proposes SkinFLNet, a privacy-preserving federated learning framework for skin cancer detection using dermoscopy images. The authors employed five deep convolutional neural networks—DenseNet169, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2—combined with fine-tuning layers to classify skin lesions. The model was evaluated on the ISBI2016 dataset, which contained 1,279 skin lesion images. Among the tested architectures, the FL-VGG16 model achieved the highest performance with 92.08% accuracy, 90.91% precision, 76.92% F1-measure, and 98.36% specificity. Additionally, the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) technique was utilized to enhance the interpretability of the model's predictions. The results suggest that this federated approach effectively balances high diagnostic accuracy with data privacy concerns in clinical settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task on the ISBI2016 dataset. The dataset contained 173 melanoma images in the original test set, which were augmented to 5232 images in the training set and 584 in the validation set.

The other class in the binary classification task on the ISBI2016 dataset. The dataset contained 727 benign images in the original test set, which were augmented to 5232 images in the training set and 584 in the validation set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or benign categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of a 'fully connected layer combined with a softmax activation function' to perform a 'classification task'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. benign) using the ISBI2016 dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, F1-measure, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISBI2016
datasets_used.description: A dataset introduced by the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) in 2016 for skin lesion analysis, containing images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It consists of dermoscopy images with varying resolutions.

use.training: Used for training the five Deep CNN models (DenseNet169, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2) after data augmentation and SMOTE balancing. The original 900 training images were augmented to 10,464 images (5,232 melanoma, 5,232 benign).
use.validation: Used for validation. The original 900 training images were split into 1,308 training and 146 validation images after SMOTE. After augmentation, the validation set consisted of 1,168 images (584 melanoma, 584 benign).
use.testing: Used for testing the final models. The dataset contained 379 test images (75 melanoma, 304 benign).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, specificity, and F1-measure.

VGG16.notes: Minimum accuracy among non-FL models; best precision and specificity; lowest F1-measure

InceptionV3.notes: Results not explicitly quantified in text

Xception.notes: Results not explicitly quantified in text

InceptionResNetV2.notes: Results not explicitly quantified in text

ISBI2016.notes: These results were obtained without the FL environment to compare against the FL setup.

architecture_used.main_model: Five Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet169, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2) combined with a Federated Learning approach (SkinFLNet). The best performing model was FL-VGG16.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet database (over 1 million images). DenseNet169, InceptionResNetV2, and VGG16 are explicitly noted as pre-trained on ImageNet. InceptionV3 was noted as advanced based on ILSVRC outcomes.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 for VGG16 and DenseNet169; 299x299 for InceptionResNetV2. InceptionV3 and Xception input sizes are not explicitly stated in the provided text, though InceptionV3 typically uses 299x299.

base_models: InceptionV3 (Inception modules with combined convolution filters)
Xception (36 convolutional layers, 14 blocks, depth-wise separable convolutions)
fine_tuning_layers: Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer added after each pre-trained CNN classifier
Two Dropout layers (discarding 20% and 40% of neurons)
activation_functions: ReLU (used in the first dense layer)
Softmax (used in the second dense layer for binary classification)
architecture_details.federated_learning_mechanism: FedAvg method used to combine client updates on the central server. Clients download global model, train locally, and send parameter updates back without sharing data.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (Softmax activation implies Cross-Entropy loss for classification)

hyperparameters.class_balancing: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) applied to training set

training_details.training_schedule: Not explicitly reported (e.g., number of epochs, learning rate schedule)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully Connected (Dense) layers with Softmax activation
classification_or_output_head.details: Two dense layers. First layer uses ReLU. Second layer uses Softmax for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign). Output is a probability between 0 and 1.

approximate_parameters.note: Parameter counts provided are specifically for the VGG16-based architecture in Table 5.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels (for VGG16 and DenseNet169) or 299x299 pixels (for InceptionResNetV2)
Normalization of pixel values to a range of 0 to 1
Conversion of images to RGB format
Conversion of images to Numpy arrays
Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) for class balancing
Data augmentation including vertical flip, horizontal flip, shearing (0.4), rotation (90 degrees), height shift (0.2), zooming (2), and width shift (0.2)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to balance the training data by generating synthetic samples for the minority class (melanoma). Increased the training set from 900 to 1454 images, and subsequently the balanced training set from 1308 to 10,464 images after full augmentation.
Applied as a geometric transform with a probability of True (yes/no decision).
Applied as a geometric transform with a probability of True (yes/no decision).
Applied with a shearing range of 0.4 (floating-point value selected from a uniform range between 0 and 1).
Applied with a rotation angle of 90 degrees (randomly picked from a range, though 90 is specified in the training details).
Applied with a shift range of 0.2.
Applied with a shift range of 0.2.
Applied with a zoom range of 2 (image resized within a range of [1 - zoom range] to [1 + zoom range]).

conclusions: The proposed SkinFLNet framework, which combines Federated Learning with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (DCNNs), effectively addresses the challenge of classifying skin cancer while preserving patient data privacy by avoiding the need to share raw medical images across institutions.

Among the five tested architectures (DenseNet169, VGG16, InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2), the FL-VGG16 model achieved the highest performance on the ISBI2016 dataset, with 92.08% accuracy, 90.91% precision, 76.92% F1-measure, and 98.36% specificity.

The integration of fine-tuning layers and a fully connected layer with softmax activation successfully refined the models, reducing complexity and computational cost while maintaining high diagnostic accuracy.

The use of the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) technique enhanced the transparency and interpretability of the model's predictions, which is critical for gaining trust and facilitating approval among medical experts.

The federated learning approach demonstrated superior performance compared to non-federated (centralized) models, with the global model attaining a maximum accuracy of 92.08% and best F1-measure of 76.92% using VGG16 within the secure FL framework.

While the study confirms the viability of FL for privacy-preserving skin cancer detection, it acknowledges that real-world deployment faces challenges such as network latency, communication overhead, hardware constraints in hospitals, and the need for robust infrastructure to ensure real-time synchronization.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISBI2016 dataset (1,279 images), which limits the assessment of generalizability to other populations, skin types, or lesion datasets (e.g., HAM10000, ISIC2019).

The dataset is inherently imbalanced (727 benign vs. 173 melanoma in the original training set); while SMOTE and data augmentation were used to balance classes, this synthetic oversampling may introduce bias or overfitting risks not present in real-world clinical distributions.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between models.

The federated learning simulation does not account for real-world clinical deployment challenges such as network latency, communication overhead, hardware constraints, or data heterogeneity across different institutions.

Performance metrics for InceptionV3, Xception, and InceptionResNetV2 in the non-federated baseline comparison were not explicitly quantified in the text, limiting a complete comparative analysis of all tested architectures.

The model was evaluated only on binary classification (melanoma vs. benign), ignoring other clinically relevant skin lesion classes (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma) present in larger datasets like HAM10000.
While LIME was used for interpretability, the paper does not report clinical validation or expert review of the explanations, leaving the clinical utility of the XAI component unverified.
